literal: nir- (without, beyond) + guṇa (strand of prakṛti / property in the Vaiśeṣika padārtha-grid). The lazy gloss 'without qualities' presupposes that guṇa = quality, which is precisely the collapse the term resists
invariant_definition:
*Brahman* described under negation of *guṇa*. The reading of the negation depends on which sense of *guṇa* is operative — and the schools differ on which sense is operative.

Two distinct construals must be held apart (see the *guṇa* entry for the registers).

(1) **Sāṃkhya-derived construal: *guṇātīta* — beyond the three strands of *prakṛti*.** Here *nirguṇa* asserts that *brahman* is constitutively outside the tri-strand fabric of *prakṛti* (*sattva*, *rajas*, *tamas*) — that the strand-and-evolute grammar that governs the manifest order does not apply. This is the reading every Vedāntic school accepts at some level, because every Vedāntic school accepts that *brahman* is not a *prakṛti*-product. The Bhagavad-Gītā's *guṇātīta* description (Gītā 14.22–25) belongs in this register.

(2) **Advaita-technical construal: *nirupādhika* — without *upādhi*-determined differentiating predicates.** Here *nirguṇa* asserts that the predicates that arise only because *brahman* is associated with *upādhi* (cosmic *māyā*-*upādhi* in the case of *Īśvara*, individual *antaḥkaraṇa*-*upādhi* in the case of *jīva*) do not characterize *brahman* in itself. This is *not* the claim that *brahman* has no being, no character, no reality — it is the claim that the predicate-grammar of the *vyāvahārika* standpoint does not reach the *pāramārthika* substrate. *Brahman* in this construal is *svaprakāśa* (self-luminous), *cit-svarūpa* (consciousness as essence), *ānanda-svarūpa* (bliss as essence) — these are not *upādhi*-borne *guṇa*s but the *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* itself. The *neti neti* of *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* 2.3.6 is the *śruti* warrant.

The non-Advaita schools accept (1) and reject (2). They restrict the scope of *nirguṇa* by reading the relevant *śruti* passages as denying only certain classes of *guṇa* — *heya-guṇa*s (defects) and *prākṛta-guṇa*s (the strand-fabric of *prakṛti*) — while affirming *brahman*'s real *kalyāṇa-guṇa*s (essential auspicious attributes constitutive of the Lord's *svarūpa*).

per_school.definition: *Nirguṇa brahman* is the highest description of *brahman*. The negation operates on both registers simultaneously: *brahman* is *guṇātīta* (beyond *prakṛti*'s three strands) and *nirupādhika* (without the *upādhi*-borne predicates that constitute the *jīva*-*Īśvara*-*jagat* differentiations). Crucially, *nirguṇa* does NOT mean *brahman* is a propertyless logical posit; *brahman* is *cit-svarūpa* and *ānanda-svarūpa* — these are *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* (essence-defining), not *taṭastha-lakṣaṇa* (extrinsic-defining) descriptions, and not *guṇa*s in either the Sāṃkhya-strand or the Vaiśeṣika-padārtha sense. The *neti neti* method removes superimposed predications; what remains is not nothing but *anubhava*-itself.

*Nirguṇa* texts deny only *heya-guṇa*s (defects: change, suffering, ignorance, etc.) and *prākṛta-guṇa*s (the three-strand fabric of *prakṛti* that does not constitute *brahman*'s own substance). They do not deny *kalyāṇa-guṇa*s — the infinite real auspicious attributes that are constitutive of Brahman-as-Viṣṇu's *svarūpa*. Brahman is therefore *nis-prākṛta-guṇa*, *nir-doṣa*, and *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa* simultaneously. Rāmānuja insists that an attribute-less reality is *avastu* — an unintelligible posit.

*Nirguṇa* denies only *prākṛta-guṇa*s; Viṣṇu eternally possesses infinite real auspicious *guṇa*s (each itself infinite). The Advaita reading of *nirguṇa* as *nirupādhika* is rejected as part of the larger refutation of *upādhi*-doctrine in the *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana*.

*Nirguṇa* texts describe *brahman* in its impersonal *brahmajyoti* aspect — Kṛṣṇa's bodily effulgence — and deny *prākṛta-guṇa*s of the supreme. They do not describe Kṛṣṇa's highest reality (*svayaṃ-bhagavān*), which is saturated with *svarūpa-śakti* and is the locus of eternal *līlā*. *Nirguṇa* is therefore a partial true description of a subordinate aspect, not the highest description.

translator_note: 'Without qualities' and 'attributeless' are the standard English glosses; both are defective and should be retired in serious work. They presuppose *guṇa* = 'quality' (a Western analytic predicate), and they then make *nirguṇa* sound like the bare logical posit 'a thing with no properties whatever' — which no Vedāntic school, including Advaita, defends. The correct policy: leave *nirguṇa* untranslated; on first use specify which sense of *guṇa* is being negated (the *prakṛti*-strands, in *guṇātīta*-style usage; the *upādhi*-borne predicates, in Advaita's *nirupādhika*-style usage; *heya-guṇa*s and *prākṛta-guṇa*s only, in Viśiṣṭādvaita and Dvaita). Acceptable English glosses include 'beyond the three *guṇa*s', 'without *upādhi*-determined predicates', 'free of defects and *prakṛti*-strand attributes' — never the flat 'without qualities'. The user's standing complaint is that the lazy gloss has been propagated through a generation of English-language Vedānta and contaminates every downstream usage.
